data.title: Broadcom: AI Beneficiary, Growing Backlog, Cheap Valuations, And Oversold Indicators
data.description:
I previously covered Broadcom Inc. (NASDAQ:AVGO, NEOE:AVGO:CA) in October 2025, discussing how OpenAI had sustained the ongoing AI mania to new heights, with the former likely to experience a drastic increase in its AI Serviceable Addressable Market [SAM].

Combined with the growing custom AI market share with existing customers, the upsides arising from the likely to be raised consensus forward estimates, the promising technical indicators, and the growing multi-year RPOs, I had upgraded AVGO to a Buy then.

In this article, I shall discuss why I am reiterating my Buy rating for the AVGO stock, with the recent sideways trading cadence already triggering an excellent margin of safety as observed in the cheaper valuations, aided by the raised consensus forward estimates.

These developments build upon their excellent ability to monetize the ongoing AI boom across the infrastructure layer through the growing custom AI accelerator orders/backlog and across the SaaS layers through the improved pricing power/operating leverage.

AVGO Proves Its AI Beneficiary Status Across Infrastructure/SaaS Layers

AVGO 1Y Stock Price

Since my last Buy rating, many AI-related stocks have entered a much needed correction/consolidation stage after a period of extreme exuberance in October 2025 and the market wakes up to valuation risks and elevated debt risks by November 2025.

Despite so, readers must note that AVGO remains well supported at the established Q4'25 floor of $320s - with it underscoring the emergence of bullish support for the king of custom AI accelerator segment during the multi-year cloud super cycle.

The same has been reiterated by the management in the recent FQ4'25 earnings call, with the growing consolidated backlog of $162B (+47.2% QoQ) and the guidance of "$73 billion in AI backlog to be delivered over the next 18 months" offering interested investors with an excellent insight into its intermediate term top/bottom-lines.

These reasons may also be why AVGO has felt confident to offer a promising FQ1'26 overall revenue guidance of $19.1B (+35.9% QoQ/+28.1% YoY) and adj EBITDA margin guidance of 67% (+2 points QoQ/-1 YoY), with it building upon the FY2025 outperformance at +23.8% YoY and +34.8% YoY, respectively.

Much of the tailwinds are attributed to the growing AI semiconductor opportunities at FQ1'26 revenues guidance of $8.2B (+26.1% QoQ/+100% YoY), as the segment notably drives the company's renewed growth prospects across custom AI accelerators and ethernet AI switches.

Particularly, AVGO has revealed the identity of the customer whom previously ordered "$10B of orders of AI racks" in the last earnings call, namely Anthropic, with the customer notably placing another $11B order "for delivery in late 2026."

This is on top of the new "fifth XPU customer through a $1 billion order placed for delivery in late 2026," with it underscoring the growing success of its custom AI accelerator offerings, as some of its customers, Alphabet (GOOG) also monetize the TPU offerings through external customers, including Apple (AAPL), Cohere, and Safe Superintelligence [SSI].

With certain analysts already highlighting GOOG TPU's lower Total Cost of Ownership [TCO] at $78.5M against Nvidia (NVDA) H100 at $177M, based on the assumption of "1,000-chip cluster running 24/7 inference workloads at 80% utilization," it is unsurprising that rumors already surface surrounding the potential adoption of TPUs by Meta (META).

This is on top of the supposed partnership between GOOG-META to develop the "TorchTPU" initiative to "significantly reduce switching costs for companies that want alternatives to Nvidia’s GPUs" without having to "undertaking significant, extra engineering work."

This development makes sense indeed, since many AI developers supposedly already use PyTorch, "an open-source project... which is a collection of pre-written code libraries and frameworks that automate many common tasks in developing AI software" - all of which triggering long-term growth tailwinds for AVGO as GOOG's strategic TPU partner.

These reasons may also be why AVGO has hinted "for that $73 billion (of booking) over the 18 months to keep growing," thanks to their ability to absorb more orders given:

  • their current lead time of "6 months to a year,"
  • the available 2nm/3nm foundry capacity with Taiwan Semiconductor Manufacturing Company Limited (TSM) (TSMWF), and
  • the expanding new advanced packaging facility in Singapore, to address the growing backlog.

These robust infrastructure growth prospects also built upon AVGO's excellent, recurring infrastructure software business including VMware Cloud Foundation [VCF] across compute, storage, network, container and cloud management in private/public clouds.

This is with FY2025 revenues of $27.02B (+25.8% YoY) aided by the robust insights offered by the higher bookings at $10.4B in FQ4'25 (+26.8% YoY) and the consequently growing SaaS backlog of $73B (+48.9% YoY).

Combined with the growing pricing power for the infrastructure software segment at 93% in FQ4'25 (+2 points YoY) and the consequently improved operating leverage at margins of 78% (+6 points YoY), it is apparent that Enterprise demand for AVGO's VCF offerings are rather robust.

These underscore their success "in converting our enterprise customers from perpetual vSphere to the full VCF software stack subscription" along with the "completion of the integration of VMware."

These numbers lends credibility to AVGO's promising FY2026 infrastructure software revenue growth guidance at "low double-digit percentage," with these underscoring its excellent ability to monetize the ongoing AI boom through the infrastructure and SaaS layers.

AVGO's Accelerated Growth Cadence Is Cheaply Valued

The Consensus Forward Estimates

Thanks to the outsized FY2025 performance metrics, the growing backlog/AI revenues, and the promising FQ1'26 guidance, it is unsurprising that the consensus have already raised their forward estimates, with AVGO expected to report an accelerated top/bottom-line growth at a CAGR of +35.7%/+37% through FY2028.

Most importantly, the current FY2027 adj EPS estimates of $13.68 already exceed the prior estimates of $12.14 and my prior estimates of $13.30 discussed in my last article, with it lending credence to my prior conjecture surrounding the conservative consensus estimates, as observed in the consistent upgrades over the past few months.

This builds upon the growth acceleration against AVGO's 5Y historical growth profile at +21.7%/+25.2% and the 10Y growth at +24.9%/+22.5%, respectively, with it exemplifying their renewed growth prospects arising from their ability to capitalize on the multi-year cloud super cycle and the robust hyperscaler demand for custom AI accelerators.

If anything, readers must note that the ongoing AI boom and the rich cash flows/adj EBITDA generation have already contributed to the healthier balance sheet at a net debt level of $48.96B (+15.9% YoY) and a moderating net-debt-to-adj-EBITDA ratio of 1.13x in FQ4'25, compared to a year ago at 1.82x.

AVGO Valuations

These reasons are also why AVGO looks more compelling at FWD P/E non-GAAP valuations of 32.73x, down from the recent peak of 48.99x and the 1Y mean of 36.41x, thanks to the -17.9% correction from the recent 52 week heights and the ongoing sideways trading since early September 2025.

Despite the notable premium against the 5Y mean of 24.83x and the 5Y pre-pandemic mean of 13.37x, I am of the opinion that the stock remains reasonably valued here, especially when compared to its direct AI accelerator peers, including:

  • NVDA at FWD P/E non-GAAP valuations of 38.59x,
  • Advanced Micro Devices (AMD) at 53.82x, and
  • Marvell (MRVL) at 29.72x, respectively.

Based on the FWD P/E non-GAAP valuations of 32.73x and the consensus adj EPS growth estimates at a 3Y CAGR of +37%, it is apparent that AVGO remains cheap at my estimated 3Y PEG non-GAAP valuations of 0.88x.

This is compared to the sector median of 1.70x, NVDA at 0.80x, AMD at 1.18x, and MRVL at 0.65x (using the same methodology), thanks to AVGO's ongoing stock price consolidation over the past few months, their ability to capitalize on the multi-year cloud super cycle through the growing custom AI opportunities, and the consequently raised consensus forward estimates.

AVGO Remains A Great Buy

AVGO 5Y Stock Price

For now, the bulls have proven AVGO's trading floor at $320s over the past few months of challenging market developments, as the stock also grows into its previously premium valuations through the sideways trading cadence while consolidating nearer to the 50/100 day moving averages.

AVGO Technical Indicators

These reasons are also why AVGO's technical indicators appear more attractive here, as observed in the oversold RSIs and the bottoming trading volumes - all of which pointing to its robust rebound potential upon the future recovery in AI-related sentiments.

Based on the annualized FQ4'25 adj EPS performance at $7.80 (+37.3% YoY) and the current FWD P/E non-GAAP valuations of 32.73x, it is apparent that there is still a little way to go before the stock may grow into my fair value estimates of $255.20.

Otherwise, based on the consensus FY2028 adj EPS estimates of $17.55, it appears that there is an excellent upside potential of +68.7% to my long-term price target of $574.40.

As a result of the improved margin of safety arising from the sideways trading and consequently, the cheaper valuations/oversold technical indicators, and the established Q4'25 support levels at the $320s, I am of the opinion that AVGO remains highly compelling here.

Combined with their excellent ability to monetize the ongoing AI boom across the infrastructure layer through the growing custom AI accelerator orders/backlog and across the SaaS layers through the improved pricing power/operating leverage, aided by the accelerated growth opportunities arising from the multi-year cloud super cycle, I am reiterating my Buy rating for the AVGO stock here.

data.title: All I Want For Christmas Is... Power: Alphabet's AI Energy Strategy
data.description:
Introduction

One of the companies that I didn’t have the chance to break down yet here, on Seeking Alpha, is Alphabet Inc. (GOOG). I am a shareholder of this company, just like most people who invest in the stock market, and especially those who are interested in AI. Google is everywhere - it’s inside indices, ETFs, and retirement accounts, and this is one of the stocks that rallied significantly throughout this year, even though the company is enormous.

This is my first dedicated coverage of Alphabet, but I am not going to spend time explaining what Google does, because most people reading this already know that. Instead, I want to focus on where Google is heading next, what kind of company it is becoming in 2026 and beyond, and why some of the recent decisions it has made are very important for its future outcomes.

The Intersect Deal

One of the most important recent moves from Alphabet was its decision to acquire Intersect, a data center energy provider, for $4.75 billion. It is a relatively small infrastructure acquisition for a company of this size, but it was done to solve one of the biggest bottlenecks for AI that we are expected to see in the next few years, and that is energy.

In 2026 and 2027, power availability is expected to become a main constraint for AI infrastructure, and we could even enter an energy crisis, according to some estimates. Operating large-scale data centers and training AI models requires far more electricity than today’s grids were designed to handle. If you want exposure to AI without directly investing in AI software or chipmakers, by the way, energy is one of the areas that deserves much more attention. Alphabet clearly understands this, and with this Intersect acquisition, Alphabet is being proactive rather than reactive in a few years down the road.

We could see this deal coming before, as Alphabet took a minority stake in the Intersect in 2024, helping to finance its operations. Now, Google is simply completing a move it has been preparing to do. The $4.75 billion is a very low price, especially relative to what Alphabet is getting.

Intersect has around $15 billion of infrastructure either operating or under construction. Alphabet could acquire it for a third of this number because it is taking not just assets, but the responsibility of continuing to invest in and scale that infrastructure, meaning paying off the debt that Intersect had.

Intersect is a great acquisition because it has focused on a co-location model, where clean energy plants are built directly next to data centers. So Google can build a data center and a power plant side by side and power it directly. That avoids grid congestion and lowers the risk of power shortages affecting both data centers and residential customers.

Another reason why the acquisition was a smart move is that projects like this are not simple. They face regulatory problems, require a lot of land, and have a meaningful public pushback (people say it could have powered residential buildings). Intersect’s Oberon project in California is a good example of that complexity, as it was developed under the Bureau of Land Management’s Desert Renewable Energy Conservation Plan, a process that took years of coordination between different agencies and groups.

The Oberon project is the first to achieve operation through the streamlined approach under the Bureau of Land Management (BLM) Desert Renewable Energy Conservation Plan. The landmark plan was the result of six years of collaboration between the federal government, conservation groups, Native American tribes, the renewable energy industry, utilities, and members of the public, and it designated over 10 million acres of conservation and recreation lands in the southern California desert while centering renewable energy development in designated Development Focus Areas.

This can’t be replicated quickly, so by acquiring Intersect and keeping the entire team in place, Alphabet covers the most difficult parts of infrastructure development. With $15 billion of projects already operating or under construction and a targeted 10.8 gigawatts of capacity by late 2028, this gives Google a major head start with resolving energy problem.

In general, when I step back and look at what hyperscalers do, I understand why those companies are ahead everyone else. Their decisions look like a long-term chess game, where everyone is preparing several moves ahead. In my last article about CoreWeave (CRWV), I explained that Nvidia (NVDA) works with them to reduce the risk of hyperscalers fully internalizing chip production. Hyperscalers, in turn, are building their own chips to reduce dependency on Nvidia, which is also an incredibly important move. Google’s TPUs (tensor processing units) are a good example of this strategy. They will most likely never replace Nvidia, simply because Nvidia has been focused on this space for decades, but they are being successful in taking this interdependence down. It reduces risk and improves negotiating power.

At the same time, it’s interesting to observe how these companies are slowly getting into each other’s line of business. Google, which historically was dominant in advertising, is now growing very fast in cloud, AI, data centers, and infrastructure, which was not the strength of this company until now. Amazon (AMZN), which was a dominant player in cloud, is growing its advertising aggressively, effectively stepping into what Google has been doing all along. Everyone is working on chips, robotics, and energy. Tesla (TSLA) builds robots and energy infrastructure. Amazon builds robots and works on Space projects. Google builds robots, chips, cars, and now energy.

This is all not accidental, because when you run a one-million-dollar company, the sky is the limit for you, but when you run an almost 4 billion dollar enterprise, you quickly run out of industries, so you have to add more and more if you want to grow. Alphabet is one of the strongest players, and one of those that will be the most successful in its growth.

Financials

Alphabet is in a very strong financial position to make these kinds of long-term bets. The company currently holds close to $100 billion in cash and has roughly $44 billion in total debt, so it’s not leveraged. It does not need financial engineering to fund acquisitions, or add to capital expenditures, and make constant buybacks, and you want Alphabet to continue, because this management for sure knows what it is doing.

Over the past few years, Alphabet’s return on invested capital (ROIC) has climbed from below 16% in 2020 to more than 32% today. That is an exceptional level and far above its weighted average cost of capital (WACC), meaning the company adds tremendous value from each invested dollar. When a company has a metric like that, it should just continue making assumptions and placing bets, because it is clearly working out amazingly. So, Alphabet’s rising capital expenditures make sense in that context, and I personally don’t view this as a major red flag. It is true that eventually we could face an oversupply of computing power, but with Alphabet's size, this company can put that capacity to use to power and improve its own operations, and to train its own models.

In Q3 2025, revenue grew at a mid-teens pace year over year, operating income increased, and net income jumped meaningfully even after accounting for regulatory fines that pressured margins. Google Cloud continues to stand out, with strong revenue growth and expanding operating margins, which is very important because Cloud is the backbone of Alphabet’s AI ambitions.

Free cash flow remains solid despite very heavy capital expenditures. Net change in cash can move around from year to year, but Alphabet remains comfortably cash-flow positive even while investing aggressively into CapEx, and this is the only real metric I care about at the moment. Don’t get me wrong, net income and cash from operations are as well important, but there you have a lot of rules and ground to “play around” with the numbers. Accounting is very flexible these days. But net change in cash is what this company really holds in their hands after the quarter ends, and as long as it’s in a breakeven or plus after capital expenditures, I am not worried about overspending.

Valuation

Some investors argue that Google looks expensive on traditional valuation metrics. I understand that view, but I also think that sector comparisons do not really capture what Alphabet has become. The communication services sector is too broad a category to do justice to a company that combines advertising, cloud, AI infrastructure, hardware, and now energy. Whether the company is priced fairly relies on traditional metrics that are relative to the sector, but how can you compare the hyperscalers to other companies on the market, when they are those who powered the growth for the rest of the S&P and Nasdaq indices in the last few years?

When I look at Google, I focus more on growth durability and reinvestment quality than on multiples. A company of this size growing at double-digit rates is very rare, and the compounding effect of reinvesting at high returns on capital is more important to me than whether the stock looks optically cheap at any given moment. Of course, if the multiple was at Palantir’s (PLTR) levels, I would say that no matter the premium for the excellence, there is a limit to this stretch. In Alphabet’s case, with a TTM PEG ratio slightly above the sector, Alphabet is fairly valued at the moment.

Conclusion

I would not expect explosive upside from Google from here. The stock has already had a strong run, and we have seen in the past that even Google can experience sharp drawdowns when sentiment shifts or some major correction happens, like in April of 2025.

For me, Google is a long-term compounder. If you are not a shareholder and want exposure, current levels look reasonable for a long-term position. I think these levels will look really attractive a few years down the road. If you already own it, this is the kind of company I am comfortable adding gradually over time, especially on pullbacks. The balance sheet is strong, execution is strong, and the strategic moves being made today, especially around energy and infrastructure, may turn out to be decisive advantages a few years from now.

data.title: POET Technologies: Is It The Next Takeover Target After Marvell's Celestial AI Deal?
data.description:
Investment thesis

POET Technologies (POET) shares have surged more than 65% over the last month due to a number of fundamental factors linked to talk of strategic potential for the company after the acquisition of Celestial AI by custom chip giant Marvell Technology (MRVL). Even though this deal isn't directly related to POET, it has an indirect impact because the company's tech is used in Celestial AI's flagship product, the Photonic Fabric. As a result, the market participants see this deal as a key signal of the importance of POET's photonics technologies in improving data centers and upgrading their functionality for the development of the AI industry. Therefore, my rating for POET has been revised from "Hold" to "Buy," whereas MRVL remains at "Buy." For me, POET stock is still a speculative play. But the potential that's building around the MRVL and Celestial AI deal demonstrates POET's importance in creating a data center value chain. Furthermore, I believe there's a possibility of a company like POET becoming the target of MRVL's next acquisition.

My past assessment of POET

From the time of my last publication on POET, the company's value has increased by +82.49%. I already mentioned in the introduction to this article the main reason for this growth: Possible strategic potential for the company from the completion of Marvell Technology's acquisition of Celestica AI. Sure, you could say this growth is a result of "irrational optimism." But it’s not without reason as market players who already believed in POET’s photonics tech now have a bigger reason to think they were right. Even though I was more skeptical about POET shares before, that was a well-reasoned assessment, primarily based on the results of a fundamental analysis of the company's financial profile and the risks that affect its strategic growth potential. And the main idea behind my "Hold" recommendation for POET shares was the need to wait for the period of time when the company would report its first larger orders from customers, allowing it to generate the necessary cash flow from its operating activities.

What are the details of the deal, and what does it mean for Marvell?

During its presentation of its quarterly results for Q3 2025, Marvell Technology announced the conclusion of a deal to acquire Celestial AI for $3.25 billion. This company is a big deal because of its flagship product, Photonic Fabric, that helps MRVL improve connectivity in next-gen data centers. The expanded tech capabilities of Marvell should be a plus for working with customers (data centers) building their infrastructure using custom chips. And it's Celestial AI's technology that makes the overall functionality of data centers using MRVL products more energy efficient and faster in data transfer. In my opinion, this is the main competitive advantage that reflects the quality of solutions offered by companies working on the development of digital infrastructure for the AI industry.

The importance of this deal for Marvell and the future of POET can be understood better by looking at how Photonic Fabric works. The foundation of Photonic Fabric is optical connections that make data transfer faster and more reliable. It's a next-gen tech that totally gets rid of the tech issues with traditional electrical signals. With increased data transfer speed and reliability, there's also an improvement in the energy efficiency of computing processes in data centers. Together with the optical connection, Marvell receives specialized processors (XPUs) from Celestial AI, developed to solve problems related to improving the performance of AI applications.

Given that Marvell is one of the two major players in the global custom chip market (alongside Broadcom), acquiring XPU enables it to create a more competitive offering for data center customers. The custom MRVL chips are less expensive and position the company among the leading suppliers in the AI industry. Since power consumption is one of the key barriers to the development of the new digital economy, energy efficiency technologies are key tools for product improvement. This is why the acquisition of Celestial AI is so important for MRVL, as it essentially offers a "gold mine" for future cash flow.

The importance of POET technology for Marvell's development

Given that Marvell is acquiring Celestial AI in part to obtain next-generation optical connectivity, it's also interested in the technologies that underpin the development of this product. Offering optical interconnect solutions, POET creates photonic solutions at the chip level. It does so by integrating photonics and electronics for vertical market applications. These innovations enable stable operation of networks used in AI technologies. This is essentially the first technology that allows the integration of photonics and electronics into a single device. With the rapid development of data centers, this capability will offer the market a unique solution that will increase stability and data transfer speeds. And most importantly, make it cheaper by reducing energy consumption.

The advantage of the POET optical interposer is the ability to build optical data transmission mechanisms based on light in such a way as to make it as economical and efficient as possible, minimizing manufacturing and energy costs. All of the product is formed on a single chip, allowing for the minimization of space in data center equipment. These advantages of POET technology make it possible to scale data centers to large volumes, increasing the economic efficiency of photonic solutions many times over. For more details on the features of POET technology, I discussed them in my previous article.

How does POET benefit from this?

The successful integration of Marvell with the Celestial AI platform will lead to the successful application of optical interconnect technologies. It will significantly increase bandwidth and data transfer rates, both of which play a key role in AI computing. Analyzing the relationship between Celestial AI and POET, we should note that the two companies have formed a strong technological partnership. Within the framework of the development of the Photonic Fabric platform, the POET technology is the basis for Celestial AI's developments in the field of integrating photonics into custom chips. Thanks to this, it's possible to create the high-speed solutions necessary for the operation of AI applications and data centers.

Basically, Celestial AI and POET are in the same business, just in different areas. So, POET develops tech that makes it possible to speed up data transfer using photonics, but Celestial AI already uses this tech in their systems, directly integrated into working data centers. Beginning in 2022, POET is mentioned in Celestial AI's reports, albeit as a customer to whom the technology was supplied. Based on Celestial AI's innovations, a joint development project was carried out to create a platform of optical interposers, allowing the company to offer the market a cutting-edge solution for high-speed computing.

Now that Marvell has announced its intention to acquire Celestial AI, MRVL will gain access to POET-dependent technologies through this deal. With Marvell becoming an IT giant, finding the best solutions for developing its specialized microchips, which are widely used in data centers, has become a priority. Since POET offers a specific solution that Celestial AI has implemented, this creates conditions that allow POET itself to offer its technologies to solve MRVL's problems.

Why is Marvell the future of the AI and data center industry?

In order to understand Marvell's competitive advantage in today's AI and data center industry, we need to look at the differences between its business model and that of the industry leader, Nvidia. There's a fundamental difference in their approaches to chip design. Whereas Nvidia creates universal chips with multitasking and multifunctional capabilities, Marvell's custom chips are highly specialized and designed to perform routine, repeatable tasks. AI's technological boom is impacting the semiconductor and microchip industry due to the need to meet demand from new data centers. Every day, large-scale routine tasks are performed within their structure. In order to ensure that these tasks are completed, custom chips are used, since they're cheaper and easier to manufacture.

Because of this, many cloud tech giants, such as Google (GOOG) (GOOGL) and Amazon (AMZN), have turned to custom chip manufacturers to tailor chips to their own algorithms built into their software. This allows these customers to achieve high levels of performance in routine data center tasks and, at the same time, reduce the cost of microchip technology. For this reason, it's impossible to put Marvell on the same competitive footing as Nvidia, since, although they have the same customers, the orders are completely different. Essentially, MRVL is a contractor for large IT giants in the development of their own digital infrastructure for data center operations.

Speaking of the market volume of custom chips used in the construction of data centers, in 2025, sales will amount to $55.2 billion. Analysts estimate an average annual growth rate of 12.6% for the period up to 2032, bringing sales to $126.9 billion. Considering the cyclical growth of the custom chip market and the expected increase in orders from data centers, Marvell is likely to see record growth.

Risks of the investment thesis

Analyzing the risks of the investment idea, let's split them into two parts. Firstly, because my "Buy" recommendation for POET shares depends on MRVL's growth, we should consider the threat factors that could negatively affect Marvell Technology's strategy. One of the main risks is the actions of its competitors, resulting in the launch of new custom chips on the market. It's particularly worth paying attention to Nvidia's design developments because the company may offer its new product among custom chips, making competition between Marvell and Broadcom more intense. Nvidia's successful actions would necessitate a revision of the positive assessment of MRVL's growth prospects. But this may not be as important for POET. Another risk, however, the dependence of chip sales on capital investment cycles for AI and cloud technology, poses a more significant threat.

In this case, it's important to note that MRVL and POET face the same risk. In the event of a deterioration in the macroeconomic environment, R&D and data center construction may decline. News like this, and even ordinary rumors, is viewed very negatively by the market, because excessive speculation surrounding the AI boom leads to increased fears that it could all turn out to be a bubble. If this happens, stocks of companies such as MRVL and POET will be recommended for sale rather than purchase.

Assessing the risks for POET separately, it's worth paying attention to two issues: Insider sales and financial problems. For Q3 2025, the company reported a GAAP loss per share of $0.11. The main challenge for POET is the barriers to commercialization of optical modules and light sources. But the company has already announced that it got a production order worth over $5 million to supply optical engines, and this is the first sign of big orders from customers. In terms of insider sales, there were a number of large sales between Oct. 2, 2025, and Nov. 12, 2025. Among them was the sale of 20.32 million shares by CEO Suresh Venkatesan on Oct. 2, 2025.

To me, this seems strange, considering that the company has significant growth potential in view of the potential conclusion of new deals with Marvell itself. There's an explanation for these sales, though, related to prearranged sales of shares to attract additional financial capital. What's more, these sales took place during a period when POET had previously experienced sharp growth. Taking into account that on Oct. 27, 2025, it entered into securities purchase agreements with institutional investors for 20.69 million common shares, raising approximately $150 million in proceeds, the company's total projected cash position exceeded $300 million. Insider sales are part of this transaction.

Conclusion

How can we sum this up? For starters, that POET stock surge we've seen over the last month is just speculation and shows how irrational investors can be. But we cannot ignore the fact that the deal between Marvell and Celestial AI is an opportunity that is also available to POET. Earlier, cooperation between Celestial AI and POET was a path to qualification. Indeed, it was Celestial AI that was the technology partner that could take and test POET prototypes for their professional suitability and commercial viability. Thanks to this, the potential for concluding large contracts in the future is being formed, the subject of which will be the supply of, for example, 800G/1.6T transceivers for data centers. At the moment, production orders for POET amount to $5.6 million. It's not a large amount, yet it's a certain amount of orders, demonstrating that testing is underway and appears to be successful. If this were not the case, POET and Celestial AI's collaboration would have ended earlier, instead of continuing for the fourth year.

Furthermore, it's my opinion that the successful commercialization of POET technology, as part of further testing for professional suitability in the presence of production orders, provides an additional opportunity to promote the business in the interests of IT giants. Effective solutions that reduce the energy consumption of data transmission, speeding it up and making it more reliable in data centers, is an attractive product that many companies in the AI industry need. And Marvell is one of the key players in this industry, striving to control the technologies developed for data centers. So, even if POET tech becomes the "gold standard" in the foreseeable future, Marvell might be interested in buying it up.

Importantly, it should also be noted that POET's current valuation is relatively small (market capitalization of approximately $1 billion) when compared to other acquisitions in this industry, thus making the deal more feasible. Nevertheless, in my opinion, such a transaction is unlikely to occur until the successful launch of mass production, expected in 2026. So until POET reports a transition to a new level of operational activity, any talk of such a deal will remain speculation. However, once this scenario becomes realistic and is put into practice, the price of POET shares could rise significantly. Until more realistic rumors emerge, growth could already be quite impressive. As a result, my recommendation for POET shares is upgraded from "Hold" to "Buy." That said, I will make my purchases once a certain technical correction has taken place. Considering that the current share price is around $7.5, I would like to see a correctional decline to the $5-$5.50 range. At this price level, I would be ready to purchase POET shares for long-term holding until at least the end of 2026.

data.title: Coherus Oncology: A Two-Pronged March With Mid-2026 Implications
data.description:
Topline Summary and Update

Coherus Oncology (CHRS) is a small-cap oncology company that has given me optimism, particularly as they have leveraged asset sales to fund ongoing research while also maintaining a commercial pipeline, as I discussed in my last article on the company. However, a lack of profitability and a need for riskier wins create a lot of uncertainty about the investment thesis, and I view this stock as a highly volatile play worth continuing to watch closely. Today, I want to lay out the reasons why recent updates continue to justify a "Buy" sentiment for me, despite the risks.

Pipeline Updates

Toripalimab

At this time, the only agent that is being marketed by CHRS is the PD-1 antibody toripalimab, which has one approval for patients with advanced nasopharyngeal carcinoma, a rather rare cancer in the United States. It is more common in countries like China, but Junshi Biosciences owns the rights to sell the drug there.

We have not seen much in the way of a potential label expansion for toripalimab on its own, although CHRS did recently announce six-year follow-up from the JUPITER-02 study, continuing to showcase the ability of toripalimab, when combined with chemotherapy, to provide durable disease control and a survival benefit. This data strongly reinforces the new standard of care that has been established in the setting of recurrent or metastatic nasopharyngeal carcinoma.

Further developmental efforts are focused on the use of toripalimab as a partner therapy for CHRS's other pipeline agents, discussed below.

Casdozokitug

The main developmental candidate in CHRS's pipeline is the interleukin-27 (IL-27) blocker casdozokitug. We have not seen a meaningful clinical trial update for this agent to date, but management has continued to guide that a data readout for a phase 2 trial combining casdozokitug, toripalimab, and bevacizumab in hepatocellular carcinoma (the most common form of liver cancer) is coming by mid-2026.

In early November, CHRS did present a poster at the Cytokines meeting highlighting correlative biomarker data from patients receiving casdozokitug suggesting that the agent is indeed blocking IL-27, and a triplet regimen similar to the one being explored in hepatocellular carcinoma appears to be well tolerated and associated with antitumor activity.

CHS-114

The CCR8 antibody CHS-114 continues to be evaluated in early-stage clinical studies, as well. In particular, at the SITC 2025 meeting, CHRS presented a poster showing that CHS-114 could selectively deplete CCR8-positive regulatory T cells, which was associated with immune activation in patients with head and neck squamous cell carcinoma.

CHRS is continuing to work on dose optimization studies for CHS-114 in second-line head and neck, as well as gastric cancer, with guidance of a 1H 2026 readout. A study combining toripalimab and CHS-114 in esophageal squamous cell carcinoma is also ongoing, with a first readout anticipated in mid-2026.

Financial Overview

At the end of Q3 2025, CHRS held $103.4 million in cash and equivalents, as well as $88.3 million in marketable securities. They also had $36.9 million in a non-current term loan. Revenue for the quarter was $11.6 million ($29.4 million for the first 9 months of 2025), up from $6.1 million in the same period of 2024. Meanwhile, operating expenses were $55.9 million. After interest, the net loss for the quarter was $44.5 million, down from $47.6 million in Q3 2024.

For the first 9 months of 2025, CHRS used $118.8 million in cash to fund operations. At this burn rate, the company has an operational runway of between 4 and 5 quarters. This is consistent with management guidance that they should be able to fund operations "through 2026."

Strengths and Risks

CHRS is setting up for an important year, with modest growth of their revenues fueling research into novel immunotherapy approaches. There is a lot of potential here, but there are some key challenges worth pointing out, as well.

Strength - Leveraging an approval to fund new research

First and foremost, CHRS has a very active pipeline, with notable milestones being guided for mid-2026. This is a lot of momentum building that could translate into market appreciation, most likely after Q1 2026, in my view, since the company has not guided any significant events to be expected for the first quarter.

Furthermore, toripalimab sales are continuing to grow, although there is a ceiling for this in the United States, where nasopharyngeal cancer is diagnosed in only 1 in 100,000 people, and many of these will not require systemic therapy with toripalimab. The entire global market is valued at only a bit over $1 billion per year. I continue to expect that toripalimab alone will not be able to drive profitability, but it's an important springboard for their research, and CHRS has indeed managed to continue growing their sales.

As for these newer projects, CHRS is working on some important programs in the liver cancer and head/neck cancer spaces, and phase 2 readouts could offer real signals about the future of these projects. And there are some preliminary signals that drugs like casdozokitug are active. I eagerly await more updates in this line.

Risk - Dwindling cash reserves place strong pressure on unproven pipeline candidates

Unfortunately, we should not ignore the risks posed for early-stage clinical pipeline candidates. There are some interesting signals of activity and good tolerability so far for their IL-27 and CCR8 blockers, but these are far from definitive. And unfortunately, with only around a year of cash in the tank, I would expect that CHRS is really relying on positive readouts to create leverage for an equity raise.

That fundraising effort is coming within the next year, I believe. And the key question is going to be whether it comes from a position of strength or not.

Bottom-Line Summary

Nothing has fundamentally changed for me with respect to the main investment thesis of CHRS. To wit: they're growing their sales of a commercial entity that has a pretty good lock on a small indication, but it will not be enough to drive profitability. Furthermore, their clinical pipeline is made up of 2 rather promising early candidates, for which we have yet to see a definitive data readout.

When you take this together with the arguably short cash runway, it's easy to see why the market is valuing CHRS at just about their cash and equity holdings (market cap of $169 million as I write this). Clearly, if CHRS can showcase a clear win with their mid-2026 data readouts, there is strong potential for upside here, which is the main support for my continued "Buy" sentiment.

However, in the meantime, I suspect there is going to be continued downward pressure on the stock price as CHRS has not guided any important Q1 2026 data readouts. So there may be a better entry point. With average daily volume of 1.1 million shares (relative to over 100 million shares outstanding), entering and exiting a position in CHRS could be challenging, as well, making this an especially risky trading vehicle.

In summary, I continue to affirm a "Buy" rating here based on the promise of CHRS's clinical pipeline, which is not being factored into the market's current valuation. The risk is still very high here, and we can expect a lot of volatility, but there's also the chance that CHRS is able to show a clear signal of a next step for their pipeline, and I'm very interested to continue following.

data.title: 2026 Market Outlook: Why The S&P 500 Is Entering A Frontier Phase
data.description:
Thesis

The backdrop for my 2026 S&P 500 (SP500) outlook is a market gradually waking up to space as a real frontier, as AI and automation push costs down, pull capital in, and stretch the growth runway for U.S. equities a lot farther than it used to be.

Why This Cycle Doesn’t Look Like the Last One

The economy usually follows a familiar cycle: slow at first, then improving, then strong, and eventually overheating before slowing again. For as long as I’ve worked in this field, especially late in the year, people have tried to figure out where we are in that cycle by asking one simple question: are we just getting started, or are we near the end?

What’s different now is that those orderly pieces, at least to me, are no longer lining up in that familiar order. Profits might look strong in the stock market, but businesses aren’t necessarily investing in the usual way and government policies are no longer moving in the typical order. When the pieces stop lining up the way they used to, the cycle labels lose some of their value.

That mindset naturally pushes me to look at the S&P 500 as a broad dashboard of large U.S. companies, and looking at earnings is still the most straightforward way to understand how Corporate America is actually dealing with today’s economic environment. Based on FactSet’s bottom-up estimates, which combine forecasts from sell-side analysts, earnings for the S&P 500 are expected to increase by 15% in calendar year 2026, and net profit margins are estimated to be about 13.9%. If that happens, it would be the highest annual profit margin FactSet has recorded since 2008.

This strength is not limited to just the top mega-cap companies. FactSet points out that the other 493 companies in the S&P 500 are expected to grow earnings by 12.5% in 2026, and all eleven sectors are projected to see earnings growth compared with the prior year. So, returning to the question of where we are, this looks nothing like a typical late-cycle phase, when earnings strength usually shrinks to a handful of companies, profit margins begin to sag, and analysts can be relied upon to start trimming their forecasts.

Government and central bank actions also don’t follow the usual rules; earlier this month, the Federal Reserve lowered the federal funds target range to 3.5–3.75% and under normal circumstances, when inflation is still a concern, the Federal Reserve would keep interest rates high or raise them even further. Interest rate cuts usually happen only when the economy is clearly struggling or when inflation is already under control. But here, the Fed lowered its main interest rate a bit even while admitting that inflation hasn’t fully gone away and that the job market is only starting to cool, not collapsing.

Under these conditions, corporate behavior carries more weight, and the best evidence is how much companies are spending on long-term investments. Goldman Sachs says hyperscalers spent $106 billion on capital expenditures in the third quarter, up 75% from a year earlier and projections for the top five hyperscalers now often exceed $600 billion next year, the majority of that spending, around three quarters, will go to artificial intelligence.

The Return of Frontier-Style Growth

When people discuss artificial intelligence, they nearly invariably think of life on Earth. That’s understandable, because Earth is what we know. But from an investor’s perspective, it is more outward, about what AI could do beyond Earth, especially in space, and this, to me, and maybe to you too, begins to feel like a moment in history when people first realized there was an entire continent across the ocean, something so large that it permanently expanded what humanity could imagine doing.

From time to time, markets start to clearly see a new kind of opportunity, something that can be built, funded, insured, protected, and expanded over time. That’s how past periods of major growth became worth investing in long before profits were easy to spot. The first real sign is in how money gets committed and how long investors are willing to wait, with people accepting projects that take several years to develop because the potential payoff looks big enough to be worth the wait.

Space: the final frontier…to explore strange new worlds, to seek out new life and new civilizations, to boldly go where no man has gone before. -Captain James Tiberius Kirk, Star Trek

Space, especially Mars, lunar development, and off-Earth infrastructure, is starting to fall into that category right now. That idea only works if capital is actually moving in that direction, and right now, it is:

  • Commercial activity accounts for ~78% of the global space economy
  • Total U.S. civil + defense space spending ~$77B (2024)
  • U.S. Space Force FY 2026 budget request ~$39.9B (incl. mandatory funding)
  • NASA Artemis II crewed lunar mission scheduled for early 2026
  • NASA human exploration and lunar programs funded at tens of billions annually
  • U.S. Space Development Agency awarded $3.5B for 72 defense satellites
  • Starlink constellation buildout estimated at $10B+ in capital investment
  • Starlink satellites in orbit: 9,357 total, 9,347 operational (Dec 19, 2025)
  • Data center dealmaking ~$61B in 2025 through November (record pace)
  • Defense space spending increasing globally as space becomes a contested domain
  • China and EU scaling launch, satellite, and lunar programs

For the S&P 500, you don’t have to guess when, or even if, people will ever live on Mars. This moment is more like Genoa in the 1500s, when trade and wealth grew not from the New World itself, but from the ships, ports, financiers, and merchants preparing for it. The gains today come from that same kind of buildup and anticipation and flow to everyday companies working behind the scenes.

What This Means for the S&P 500

All the excitement about space is already starting to show up in the S&P 500, and it’s causing companies to rethink how they plan to grow over the long term.

At Italian Tech Week 2025, Jeff Bezos said:

In the next kind of couple of decades, I believe there will be millions of people living in space. That’s how fast this is going to accelerate.

Meanwhile, the index is no longer made up of just traditional technology giants. Instead, it includes a range of companies that are getting ready for business opportunities beyond Earth. A good example is the hyperscalers, the large cloud computing companies such as Amazon (AMZN), Google (GOOG), and Microsoft (MSFT). They are spending billions of dollars on artificial intelligence infrastructure and a large part of that spending is going toward AI tools that could automate tasks ranging from satellite operations to designing habitats on Mars. And it doesn't stop there.

What’s compelling is how this also bleeds into defense contractors. Companies like Lockheed Martin (LMT) and Northrop Grumman (NOC) are getting ready for possible conflicts in space, and at the same time, the U.S. Space Force is planning to spend nearly $40 billion next year. That money is going toward satellites, artificial intelligence–based systems to spot threats, and lunar programs. All of this creates demand for other industries too, including companies that supply rare earth materials and construction technology. Also, factories in microgravity can produce extremely pure crystals or advanced medicines that are difficult or impossible to make on Earth. The benefits from this reach back to Earth-based industries, including healthcare, semiconductor manufacturing, and energy companies that are part of the S&P. And while this all happening, China and the European Union are expanding their own space programs, which increases competition and ends up giving U.S. companies an extra push to grow.

Taken together, these developments show that space is no longer a niche government venture, it’s becoming a core economic engine as the global space economy reached $613 billion last year, with commercial activity making up 78% of that total, and it's growing at about 8% per year. Essentially, for the S&P, this translates into stronger earnings across more companies.

Beyond near-term earnings, the space sector is beginning to behave like a general-purpose growth driver that spreads through the economy, similar to how the internet did in the 1990s. In other words, investors are putting money in now and are willing to wait several years because the potential returns could propel portfolios into a higher orbit.

Space isn’t taking growth away from businesses on Earth, it’s giving them more room to grow. Artificial intelligence changes that by letting machines operate, decide, and optimize both with, and without humans in the loop. By 2026, this expansion into space could lead investors to value these companies more highly, as markets begin to factor in many decades of additional growth. In that sense, Corporate America is preparing for liftoff into its next growth frontier.

S&P 500 Year-End 2026 Price Target

What this really means is we're looking at an S&P 500 that could hit 8,200 by the end of 2026. Bottom-up estimates peg 2026 EPS at around $310 with 15% year-over-year growth. If investors are willing to pay about 26.5x those future earnings (a level that makes sense given the productivity gains from AI and relatively low interest rates) that math supports an 8,200 level. Right now, the average Wall Street target is closer to 7,968, which would be about a 15.5% gain from today, but that estimate seems on the cautious-side.

Investments in space-related technology add that extra boost. When investors see sustained, multi-year capital deployment, valuation ceilings tend to rise. Space investment fits that pattern. Large cloud companies are projecting more than $500 billion in capital spending in 2026, much of it tied to AI and space technology. Again, this is the frontier effect. It’s similar to what happened during the railroad boom, where early spending created entire business ecosystems, a comparison also popularized by writers like Derek Thompson. As more people understood what was happening, public interest grew, more money flowed in, and company values increased.

The same pattern is playing out now. A SpaceX IPO (SPACE) next year would be one of several major, paradigm-shifting events that could help the American public truly understand and accept how much the space economy will matter over the next few decades.

Risks: Where the Frontier Thesis Can Break

First off, geopolitics could turn space into a battleground. Tensions between the United States, China, and Russia are already increasing, as space-related weapons, such as signal jammers and anti-satellite missiles, are being developed and improved very quickly. China’s plans to expand its activities on the Moon and Russia’s recent tests have caused concern because they could interfere with satellites, systems essential for moving large amounts of data used by artificial intelligence. If tensions turn into open conflict, the supply chains for rare earth materials and space launch technology could be disrupted. That would negatively affect companies listed in the S&P index, including defense contractors and semiconductor companies.

Add cybersecurity concerns as well: in 2025, hacking attacks linked to space systems increased by 118%. Many of these attacks came from state-backed groups and hacktivists.

Even without geopolitical shocks, investor expectations themselves could become a source of risk. Some experts worry that AI is being overhyped, similar to what happened during the dot-com bubble and right now, trillions of dollars are being invested in AI development. If artificial intelligence fails to deliver strong results or profits quickly, large companies that run massive cloud platforms could pull back on spending. That kind of slowdown in investment would look a lot like the technology crash we saw almost a quarter century ago.

Finally, S&P 500 values are already heavily concentrated in a small group of extremely large corporations, which makes the overall market more fragile than it appears.

None of this suggests a straight-line future. And history, which includes many unexpected “black swan” events, reminds us that markets almost never move in a straight line.

Final Takeaway

Based on this outlook, I would rate the S&P 500 as a buy. I see a rare combination of broad-based earnings growth, expanding profit margins, accommodative monetary policy, and a credible new long-cycle growth driver in AI and space-related infrastructure. At this point, the greater danger is thinking this growth phase will end too soon, not realizing how long it could continue.

data.title: Sphere Entertainment: High Short Interest And A Promising Outlook
data.description:
Investment thesis

Although the share price of Sphere Entertainment Co. (SPHR) has grown dramatically in the recent past, there is a substantial short interest, enough to concern many investors.

In this article, we review some of the issues that might lead investors to sell short rather than buy. In addition, we consider some of the positive signs coming from the company and from consumer markets.

I rate Sphere a Hold.

About Sphere

In its Q3-2025 earnings report, the company describes itself a “leader in immersive entertainment, technology and media.” It has what it calls a “next generation” entertainment medium, one that uses technology and especially artificial intelligence [AI] to provide new, immersive experiences.

The firm operates through two segments: Sphere and MSG Networks. The former operates a major entertainment facility in Las Vegas, Nevada and has plans for another venue in Abu Dhabi. This segment is having a major success with its Wizard of Oz immersive experience movie in Las Vegas, a new show that has sold 1.5 million tickets in just a few months.

The latter is a new iteration of the Madison Square Garden corporation, which operates two sports and entertainment networks. Financial results from this segment have been disappointing for several reasons. It receives most of its revenue from cable and satellite providers and those providers have been declining every year. At the same time, the fees it pays to get professional sports content is rising.

Short interest

On December 19, 2025, Seeking Alpha had this data on short interest:

While short interest has increased substantially this year, it has recovered somewhat since peaking earlier this year.

Still, short interest of more than mid-single or even high single-digits should raise alarms, and 24.69% is well above both of those levels.

I believe there are several reasons that might explain the high level. They include its valuation, the weak performance of MSG Networks, the capital intensity of organic growth, and competition.

Valuation

The price has jumped 153.44% in the past year, and that signals valuations are likely to be high. The data bears out that opinion. Price/Sales TTM, for example, receives a D grade from the Seeking Alpha system because of a 3.15 ratio, well above the Communication Services sector median of 1.19. Price/Sales FWD comes in at 2.75, more than double the sector median of 1.22.

What is perhaps more concerning is that the P/S ratio has been growing steadily over most of the past year:

Price/Book TTM stands at 1.83, just above the sector median of 1.74. Price/Cash Flow TTM has a ratio of 40.10 versus 7.14 for the sector, earning it a grade of F.

Weak performance at MSG Networks

On the Q3 earnings call, James Dolan, the Executive Chairman and CEO said, “As far as networks goes, the -- we were able to put -- to pay down some of the debt again. So that was -- that was good. They -- look, regional sports is a very powerful product. The problem in that business is that because of the shift from linear over to streaming, right? The monetization of that product, right, has took a real hit. And it's still figuring out its way.”

On the same call, CFO Robert Langer reported that MSG Networks had lost 13.5% of its subscribers between Q3-2024 and the same quarter this year.

Capital intensity

The following table, using figures from previous annual reports, shows capital expenditures in each of the past five years (note: there are two year-ends in 2024):

Presumably, the new Sphere facility in Abu Dhabi will also require substantial amounts of capex. And, if management proceeds with smaller footprint theaters in other cities, more capital will be needed for them. Judging by the success of the Las Vegas venue, though, other venues could generate significant profits.

On the MSG side, the company does not have facility costs, but buying rights can be capital intensive. These contracts are fixed costs and stay the same even when subscriber counts are falling (the so-called ‘cord cutting’ phenomenon).

Competition

While Sphere offers a unique experience and has enjoyed strong ticket sales, it is just one piece of a fragmented industry—competing for consumer discretionary dollars. Not only are there other experiential venues in Las Vegas, but there are other experiential forms of entertainment, including IMAX Corporation (IMAX) theaters, theme parks, virtual reality [VR] tools, and at-home entertainment.

MSG has a strong base in New York and the Tri-State area, but it is a regional sports network and thus faces strong competition from the national sports networks such as ESPN. Some leagues, including the NBA and NHL now offer direct access to some games. Amazon.com, Inc. (AMZN) now offers sports through Prime, and other attractions compete for some of the same consumers.

Positive signs

Those are some of the numerous reasons why investors might short sell Sphere stock. But there are also positive signs indicating the firm could be a winner for shareholders.

In the Q3 earnings report, the company reported outstanding results for The Wizard of Oz show. It opened on August 28, and by mid-October had sold one million tickets (updated in early December to 1.5 million). Based on early indications, The Wizard may become an established, long-running, and profitable show, not only in Las Vegas but also in other areas where it builds theaters.

Management also reported that Sphere had reached multi-year sponsorship agreements with Zoox (ZOOX) and Lenovo Group Limited (LNVGY).

On the financial side, the firm repurchased roughly $50 million worth of its Class A common stock in Q3, which it billed as a sign of confidence in its long-term growth potential.

Some of that confidence may come from the recent uptick in net income and normalized basic EPS, although the Q3 increase in net income was not as strong as the gain in Q3 EPS:

Several analysts have upgraded or raised their price targets of Sphere in the recent past. Prominent among them is Morgan Stanley (MS), which likes what it is seeing from The Wizard of Oz and upgraded its rating from “equal weight” to “overweight”. It also raised its target price to $105.00 from $75.00.

Valuation

To set the stage for valuation ratios and grades, this is a chart of Sphere’s prices since it began to trade publicly in 2020:

As noted, the price jumped 153.44% in the past year. For the valuation measures that are available, it receives an overall D+ grade. Price/Sales, both TTM and FWD, get D grades while Price/Book ratios, TTM and FWD get B-minus and B grades respectively. Some common ratios are unavailable because the firm has no positive earnings.

Looking forward, the Wall Street analysts expect earnings will take a big step back in 2026 before gains in 2027 through 2029, and profitability in 2030:

If earnings do drop as far as predicted in 2026, then I would expect Sphere shares to fall as well.

What we’re left with is a figurative tug-of-war between expected lower earnings and growing revenue as the firm enjoys a full year of Wizard revenue.

I end up seeing the tug-of-war as a draw, and my one-year target price will be about the same as it was at the close on December 19: $93.24.

With that target and the pros and cons listed above, I rate Sphere a Hold. There have been two other Seeking Alpha ratings in the past 90 days, both Buys.

The Wall Street analysts have an average one-year target of $87.10, which would be a 6.59% decline. However, don’t take that target in isolation. They also have five Strong Buys, two Buys, three Holds, and one Strong Sell. They have two Up revisions and one Down revision as well.

As we saw above, Morgan Stanley has raised its target price to $105.00. The Quant system has a Hold rating.

Risk factors

As listed in the Short Interest section above. Plus:

Getting Sphere to the point where it could start earning revenue was a costly business, as the table of capital expenditures showed. As a result, the company said in its 10-K for 2024 that it has “substantial indebtedness” and is highly leveraged.

The company disclosed in the 10-K that the Dolan family controls Sphere and through that control could prevent or take actions that are contrary to the interests of other shareholders.

The firm is subject to many government regulations, at multiple levels, and in a growing number of countries. These regulations include considerations related to local permits and licenses, public health and safety, the environment, broadcast rules, and data privacy.

Conclusion

Reviewing Sphere’s positive and negative factors, I am reminded of some tech companies, including Amazon.com, that sacrificed short-term profitability for market share or other long-term benefits.

That helps me make sense of the high short interest. While there are several promising developments, there are also legitimate reasons to short the firm. As it moves forward there may be issues, such as 2026’s estimated EPS, that will detract from investors’ expectations.

Again, I expect the price to be about the same in one year as it was on December 19, 2025 and have a Hold rating.

data.title: Braze: The Rebound Is Just Beginning As Revenue Accelerates
data.description:
The stock market of 2025 has been a very volatile environment that has carved out clear winners and losers. Large-cap growth stocks have been the major source of this year's gains, while small- and mid-cap companies have been left behind in the dust. My priority for 2026, meanwhile, is to prepare for a potential rotation into more value-oriented small- and mid-cap names.

Braze (BRZE), in my view, is one of the most compelling plays. The stock has shed a lot of ground this year as investors feared that its customer engagement software would shed business in a year that many companies have cut marketing budgets, fearing a slowdown in consumer spending. This has not played out at all, as Braze's revenue growth has accelerated, with the company landing deals with larger customers. Down ~15% since the start of the year, Braze is ripe for a rebound.

I last wrote a "Buy" article on Braze in September, when the stock was trading at a modest $31 per share. Even now that Braze has jumped to the mid-$30s, I think the stock has just begun the process of re-rating its cheap valuation multiples upward, in line with the company's increasingly visible strength in signing on enterprise clients. I'm reiterating my "Buy" rating on this stock.

To me, these are the core reasons to be bullish on Braze:

  • Becoming a larger enterprise vendor. Braze's core sales strategy is to push toward upmarket clients, and the company's recent ~30% y/y growth to over 300 customers generating >$500k in ARR (annualized recurring revenue) is ample proof that this strategy is working. Also, the company is enjoying long-term commitments from its customers, as evidenced by solid RPO growth.
  • Clear AI tailwinds. The company gives its customers the ability to leverage AI tools to drive omnichannel marketing campaigns. Given the pressure on CMOs to measure ROI on advertising spend, it's clear that Braze is capitalizing on strong demand for more targeted advertising, and its acceleration in growth rates proves its appeal in the market.
  • International growth opportunities. The company is making headway in Europe and Asia, and international today represents ~45% of total revenue, with management indicating room for further expansion.
  • Clean balance sheet. Braze has over $300 million in cash against no debt, which is significant for a company of its relatively smaller scale.

Despite its strengths, we note that Braze trades at an incredible valuation multiple for a stock touting ~20% growth. At current share prices near $36, Braze sits at a market cap of $4.09 billion. Netting off the $383.3 million of cash on the company's latest balance sheet gives us an enterprise value of $3.71 billion.

For the remainder of FY26 (ending in January 2026), Braze has raised its full-year growth expectations to $730.5-$731.5 million, or 23% y/y growth, versus prior guidance calling for just 20%-21% growth previously.

For next year FY27, Wall Street is expecting the company to deliver $857.4 million in revenue, or 17% y/y growth. This positions Braze's valuation multiples at:

  • 5.1x EV/FY26 revenue
  • 4.3x EV/FY27 revenue

Meanwhile, we can stack up Braze against a wide range of other mid-teens/low ~20%s growth software companies, and few come even close to Braze's low single-digit multiple.

Braze's recent results offer a clear demonstration that the market's earlier fears that a reduction in marketing spend would crimp its growth rates are premature fears. In my view, Braze's recent rebound is just the beginning of a longer rally as the market consolidates around the company's strong performance. Stay long here and hold out for a bigger rally.

Q3 download

Let's now go through Braze's recent quarterly results in greater detail. The Q3 earnings summary is shown below:

Braze's revenue grew 25% y/y to $190.8 million, beating Wall Street's expectations of $184.0 million (+21% y/y) by a four-point margin. We have to emphasize here that Braze has been the subject of tremendous deceleration fear, especially as many other customer experience/customer engagement and marketing software platforms have seen weaker retention, especially among those companies that are geared toward smaller customers.

This weakness couldn't be further from the truth for Braze, however, which achieved 170bps of sequential growth acceleration in Q3, as shown in the chart below. In fact, Braze's 25.5% growth in Q3 was the strongest in five quarters.

Continued success upmarket is the primary catalyst behind Braze's improved growth rates. As shown below, the company added 21 net-new customers that generate over $500k in annual revenue, growing the count by 29% y/y to 303 such customers. It also added 106 net-new customers overall, more than 80 added in Q2 and only 46 added in Q1. All of these metrics further serve to highlight that the tough macro environment of 2025 and the growth of AI tools have not at all hindered Braze's growth.

Another indicator of strong customer commitments is Braze's remaining performance obligations, or essentially backlog. RPO which will be recognized as revenue in less than one year, grew 25% y/y to $573 million, while total RPO grew 24% y/y to $891 million (slightly more than a year's worth of revenue already contractually locked into backlog). The mid-20%s growth pace in RPO, lining up with the company's recent pace of revenue growth, helps to suggest that Braze's revenue growth won't decelerate anytime soon.

Management also commented on a robust pipeline, which gives us plenty of hope that the company can materially outperform Wall Street's current consensus of Braze's growth rates decelerating to 17% in 2026 (fiscal 2027). Per CEO William Magnusson's remarks on the Q3 earnings call:

Our momentum was strong in the quarter as we again realized solid bookings across verticals and geographies. Pipeline generation was solid, indicating continued market demand while customers continue to adopt more channels and AI solutions, driving optimism as we look ahead to fiscal year 2027 [...]

This power of AI to build personalized cross-channel campaigns was on display during this year's Cyber Week, running from November 25 to December 1, as marketers increasingly leverage AI to accelerate campaign creation and improve overall performance. Over the Cyber Week period, Braze delivered 102.5 billion messages with global sending throughput peaking at about 28.5 million messages per minute. During the 4-day period running from Black Friday through Cyber Monday, Braze delivered nearly 60 billion messages with 100% uptime, demonstrating the strength, scale and reliability of our platform.

On the profitability front, we note that Braze also achieved 410bps of y/y pro forma operating margin expansion to 2.7%.

Though the company has just broken past the breakeven line, we note that Braze is still an earlier-stage company with mid-20%s growth and an excellent ~70% pro forma gross margin, giving the company ample opportunities to generate economies of scale as it continues to land larger customers.

Key takeaways

Recent trading in the final weeks of 2025 suggests that the rotation to cheaper small- and mid-cap stocks is already beginning, and Braze has a lot to offer investors: low multiples, accelerating growth, improving margins, and verifiable AI tailwinds that are helping the company sign large new customers. Stay long here and keep riding the recent upside higher.

data.title: Novo Nordisk's Christmas Gift To Shareholders: The Wegovy Pill Has Been Approved
data.description:
I was losing hope regarding the oral semaglutide (aka Wegovy pill) gaining FDA approval, but the positive outcome came right before Christmas. I personally think this is the turning point for Novo Nordisk A/S (NVO), and this latest news lays the grounds for an epic turnaround in 2026.

Everything you need to know about the Wegovy pill

In 64 weeks, the Wegovy pill delivered an average weight loss of 16.60% in the OASIS 4 trial. However, focusing just on a rough number in terms of weight loss it would be scratching the surface: this pill is much more than that. Besides helping people lose body weight, this pill is able to maintain the weight reduction over the long term, on top of significantly reducing risks of major adverse cardiovascular events. In other words, this pill can improve the lives of obese people from multiple perspectives.

Yesterday, the FDA approved the first oral GLP-1 drug in the U.S., and CEO Mike Doustdar seemed pretty enthusiastic about it:

The pill is here. With today's approval of the Wegovy® pill, patients will have a convenient, once-daily pill that can help them lose as much weight as the original Wegovy® injection.

As the first oral GLP-1 treatment for people living with overweight or obesity, the Wegovy® pill provides patients with a new, convenient treatment option that can help patients start or continue their weight loss journey. No other current oral GLP-1 treatment can match the weight loss delivered by the Wegovy® pill, and we are very excited for what this will mean for patients in the US.

In all of this, where is Eli Lilly (LLY)? Well, the U.S. company is behind Novo by at least a couple of months. Orforglipron, Eli Lilly’s obesity pill, is expected to get FDA approval by March 2026. However, the Novo advantage is not just about the timing of the approval: its pill is actually more effective. In 72 weeks, Orforglipron delivered a body weight loss of 11.20%, way lower than the 16.60% in 64 weeks of the Wegovy pill.

Okay, but injectable tirzepatide (Eli Lilly) is still better than injectable semaglutide (Novo Nordisk), right? Well, I wouldn’t be so sure about it. In the STEP UP phase 3 trial, a semaglutide 7.20 mg dose provided an average weight loss of 20.70% in 72% weeks, a bit more than the 20.20% in 72 weeks of tirzepatide.

The 7.20 mg dose is not yet approved by the FDA, but thanks to the CNPV expedited program, the company expects a review in 1-2 months. Consider that Novo filed for the FDA approval on November 26, so in January 2026, it could start selling the Wegovy pill and the higher dose injectable semaglutide. They are both huge growth catalysts.

Finally, the starting dose of the Wegovy pill will cost only $149 per month, way lower than a couple of years ago if you wanted to lose weight through GLP-1 drugs. This low entry price might be a strong incentive for people who suffer from obesity and want to improve their overall health.

Why this news might be the turning point

I was long waiting for the approval and believe this might be the turning point for this company. The Wegovy pill approval lays the grounds for a comeback in 2026 for mainly two reasons:

The first one is that Novo is literally the only player in this field. It will have about 90 days of no competition, and even when Orforglipron arrives, the lower effectiveness of the latter is a significant issue for Eli Lilly.

As stated multiple times in my previous articles, statistic evidence shows that the majority of people prefer an oral drug compared to an injectable one. Syringes are much more intimidating than a pill; my opinion is that many obese people refrained from using GLP-1 drugs because of this. How many people we are talking about? I don’t know, but what I do know is that less than 1% of people with obesity are treated with branded anti-obesity medications.

For these reasons, I expect a huge demand for the Wegovy pill since the early phase of its commercialization. And this time, the company won’t make the same mistake as it did with Ozempic/Wegovy. Back then, Novo Nordisk messed up the supply chain of these drugs, and since semaglutide is crucial for people who suffer from type 2 diabetes, the FDA announced a public shortage and let compounders make these drugs without patents.

This time is different, in fact Novo Nordisk started to produce a considerable amount of Wegovy pills way before the actual approval date.

Is Novo still a buy?

The Wegovy pill news has let Novo’s price per share soar, but I think we are not even close to the full potential of this company: it is still highly undervalued.

Novo Nordisk P/E Ratio is close to its lowest level since 2003, and at the same level as the Great Financial Crisis. However, as I have shown you, the growth prospects for this company are impressive. It does deserve a price multiple at least in line with the historical average (24.89x), and since the P/E ratio is currently 14x, that would mean a 77% upside just from multiple mean reversion. And I am not considering any other factor like EPS growth, profitability improvements and EPS stability.

Since 2003, this company delivered a steadily increasing CFO per share, and managed to improve its profit margin over the years. Tell me another company with such an impressive historical performance and with a P/E Ratio of just 14x. Definitely not Eli Lilly.

The profit margin is lower than Novo Nordisk’s, and it doesn’t present the same consistency. Plus, even though in strong ascent, the CFO per share was pretty inconsistent over the last 2 decades. On top of that, the P/E ratio is 53x, more than 3 times that of Novo.

Getting back to my previous question, sure, Novo Nordisk seems still quite cheap at the current price. I won’t invest more just because I went all-in a couple of months ago and it is currently my top holding. The 2025 was a terrible year for the Danish giant, but 2026 does bode well.

Editor's Note: This article discusses one or more securities that do not trade on a major U.S. exchange. Please be aware of the risks associated with these stocks.

data.title: U.S. Growth With A Capital K
data.description:
By James Knightley, Chief International Economist, US

Net trade lifted US growth well above expectations

The delayed US third quarter GDP report has come in at an eye-popping 4.3% annualised rate, a full percentage point above the consensus expectation. This was primarily due to a strong performance from net trade, with exports rising 8.8% and imports falling 4.7%. This means that net trade contributed 1.6pp of the 4.3% headline growth rate. Other than that, consumer spending grew a robust 3.5% versus the 2.7% rate expected.
